title: Aortic Dissection
oneLiner: Acute intimal tear in the aorta – life-threatening emergency requiring immediate blood pressure control & surgical input.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Haemopericardium / cardiac tamponade:** Hypotension + muffled heart sounds + distended neck veins → bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with right heart collapse
- **Acute aortic regurgitation (severe):** Sudden onset diastolic murmur + bounding pulses + flash pulmonary oedema with normal LV function on echo
- **Coronary ostial involvement (usually right):** ECG shows inferior STEMI pattern – but chest X-ray shows widened mediastinum → DO NOT give thrombolysis
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden, severe, "tearing" or "ripping" chest pain (often migrating to back/abdomen)
  - Syncope (18-30% of cases – think of dissection even if no pain)
  - Neurological deficit (stroke, paraplegia, Horner’s syndrome)
  - Limb ischaemia (absent pulse, cold, painful limb)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension OR hypotension (sign of tamponade/rupture)
  - Asymmetric arm BP (>20 mmHg difference)
  - Diastolic murmur (AR)
  - Focal neurological deficit (hemiparesis, spinal cord syndrome)
  - Hoarse voice (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy) – rare but classic
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute myocardial infarction** – ECG: ST elevation (usually inferior if right coronary involved); single BP, not unequal; troponin rises <6 hours. *Splitter: Unequal arm BP + normal ECG → dissection, not STEMI.*
- **Pulmonary embolism** – tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, D-dimer high, CT pulmonary angiogram shows clot, not aortic flap
- **Pneumothorax** – pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, CXR shows air, not widened mediastinum
- **Pericarditis** – positional pleuritic pain, diffuse ST elevation, PR depression, normal aortic imaging
- **Musculoskeletal chest pain** – no haemodynamic instability, reproducible tenderness, normal ECG/imaging
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ECG** – rule out STEMI (but remember: inferior MI can be caused by dissection)
  - **BP both arms** – document difference >20 mmHg
  - **Chest X-ray** – look for widened mediastinum >8 cm at aortic knob, loss of aortic knuckle, pleural cap – but normal CXR does NOT rule out dissection
- **Bloods:**
  - **Troponin** – often negative early; if elevated, suggests coronary involvement
  - **D-dimer** – sensitive (if negative, low risk) but not specific
  - **Lactate** – for end-organ hypoperfusion
  - **Cross-match** – urgent for potential surgery
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT aortogram (CT angiogram with ECG-gating) – gold standard; shows intimal flap, false lumen, extension
  - **Second-line:** Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) – if patient unstable and cannot go to CT; also assesses AR and tamponade
  - **Transthoracic echo (TTE):** poor for ascending aorta but can spot AR, pericardial effusion, proximal flap
- **Never-miss test:** **CT aortogram** – omitting it in a patient with suggestive history + any abnormal finding (pain, pulse deficit, wide mediastinum) is a serious error
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** – secure IV access ×2, monitor BP
  - **Analgesia:** IV morphine 5-10 mg titrated
  - **Heart rate control first:** IV beta-blocker (labetalol or esmolol) to target HR 50-60 bpm before considering vasodilators
  - **Blood pressure control:** If SBP >120 mmHg after HR controlled, add IV vasodilator (e.g. sodium nitroprusside [check local policy] or nicardipine infusion) to target SBP 100-120 mmHg
  - **Urgent referral to cardiothoracic surgery and/or vascular surgery** – type A (involving ascending aorta) needs emergency surgery; type B (descending only) may be medical if uncomplicated
  - **DO NOT give thrombolytics** for suspected STEMI if dissection is possible
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Monitor in HDU/ICU** – strict BP control with continuous arterial line
  - **Type A:** emergency surgical repair (open or endovascular)
  - **Type B (uncomplicated):** continue medical therapy: beta-blocker + calcium channel blocker (if beta-blocker CI); keep SBP <120 mmHg, HR <60
  - **Pain control** – if pain persists, suspect rupture or extension → urgent reassessment
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Lifelong antihypertensives** – beta-blocker first-line (e.g. atenolol 50-100 mg daily), add ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5-10 mg daily) or calcium channel blocker (e.g. amlodipine 5-10 mg daily) to keep SBP <130 mmHg
  - **Regular imaging surveillance** – CT or MRI at 1, 3, 6, 12 months then annually if stable
  - **Conservative lifestyle** – avoid heavy lifting, straining, contact sports
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until surgical plan clear
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 q15min initially; once stable q1h; plus continuous BP and HR
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily – but hold if risk of bleeding (pending surgery, malperfusion)
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid at 100 mL/h unless hypotension (then consider caution – too much fluid can worsen tamponade)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Labetalol** – IV bolus 20 mg over 2 min, then 40-80 mg every 10 min up to 300 mg total, or infusion 1-2 mg/min
  - **Esmolol** – IV loading 500 mcg/kg over 1 min, then infusion 50-200 mcg/kg/min
  - **Metoprolol** – IV 5 mg every 5 min up to 15 mg (if labetalol unavailable)
  - **Sodium nitroprusside** – infusion 0.3-10 mcg/kg/min (use only after beta-blockade)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Diltiazem** IV – if beta-blocker contraindicated (e.g. asthma, cocaine use) – 0.25 mg/kg bolus, then infusion 5-15 mg/h
  - **Nicardipine** – IV infusion 5-15 mg/h (alternative vasodilator)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cocaine-induced dissection: avoid beta-blockers initially; use calcium channel blocker + benzodiazepine
  - Bradycardia/heart block: caution with beta-blockers
  - Sodium nitroprusside: risk of cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (>24h) or renal impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] – e.g. nitroprusside metabolites accumulate in renal failure; labetalol dose may need reduction in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving a vasodilator (e.g. GTN, nitroprusside) before achieving heart rate <60 bpm with a beta-blocker** – this can cause reflex tachycardia and propagate the dissection
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Rupture** – sudden hypotension, loss of pulse, death
  - **Cardiac tamponade** – due to haemorrhage into pericardium
  - **Acute aortic regurgitation** – pulmonary oedema, hypotension
  - **Myocardial infarction** – due to dissection flap occluding coronary ostium
  - **Stroke** – carotid involvement
  - **Spinal cord ischaemia** – paraplegia
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia** – abdominal pain, lactate rising
  - **Renal artery occlusion** – anuria, rising creatinine
  - **Limb ischaemia** – absent pulse, cold limb
- **Chronic:**
  - **Chronic dissection** – persistent false lumen, risk of late rupture
  - **Aneurysm formation** – up to 40% of survivors develop aneurysm
  - **Recurrent dissection** – especially in hypertensive patients
sections.Prognosis:
- **Type A:** Untreated mortality ~1-2% per hour in first 48h; emergency surgery reduces in-hospital mortality to 10-30%. Survival beyond 5 years ~60% with good BP control.
- **Type B (uncomplicated):** In-hospital mortality <10% with medical management; 5-year survival >70% if BP controlled.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check all pulses (carotid, radial, femoral, dorsalis pedis)** and **measure BP in both arms** on every patient with severe chest/back pain – asymmetry is a red flag
- **Listen for aortic regurgitation** – early diastolic murmur at left lower sternal edge – even if BP is high
- **If you suspect dissection, page cardiothoracic surgery immediately** – do not wait for all investigations to come back
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't rely on a normal chest X-ray** to rule out dissection – up to 20% of type A dissections have a normal CXR
- **Don't wait for troponin to "rule in" MI** before considering dissection – a negative troponin does not exclude dissection
- **Do NOT give thrombolysis** (for STEMI) if there is any suspicion of dissection – catastrophic bleeding
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Sudden severe recurrence of chest/back pain, syncope, new neurological symptoms (focal weakness, hoarseness), or limb pain/pallor
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient vascular/cardiothoracic clinic within 2-4 weeks; arrange surveillance CT/MRI
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (CT surgery / vascular surgery) immediately if:** Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg), acute neurological deterioration, signs of tamponade (hypotension + muffled sounds + JVP↑)
- **Call ICU:** NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS drop by ≥2 points, refractory hypertension (SBP >140 mmHg despite two agents)
- **Crash team:** Cardiorespiratory arrest – consider ED thoracotomy in selected cases (if tamponade/rupture suspected)

rawMarkdown:
## Aortic Dissection
**One-liner:** Acute intimal tear in the aorta – life-threatening emergency requiring immediate blood pressure control & surgical input.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Haemopericardium / cardiac tamponade:** Hypotension + muffled heart sounds + distended neck veins → bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with right heart collapse
- **Acute aortic regurgitation (severe):** Sudden onset diastolic murmur + bounding pulses + flash pulmonary oedema with normal LV function on echo
- **Coronary ostial involvement (usually right):** ECG shows inferior STEMI pattern – but chest X-ray shows widened mediastinum → DO NOT give thrombolysis

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden, severe, "tearing" or "ripping" chest pain (often migrating to back/abdomen)
  - Syncope (18-30% of cases – think of dissection even if no pain)
  - Neurological deficit (stroke, paraplegia, Horner’s syndrome)
  - Limb ischaemia (absent pulse, cold, painful limb)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension OR hypotension (sign of tamponade/rupture)
  - Asymmetric arm BP (>20 mmHg difference)
  - Diastolic murmur (AR)
  - Focal neurological deficit (hemiparesis, spinal cord syndrome)
  - Hoarse voice (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy) – rare but classic

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute myocardial infarction** – ECG: ST elevation (usually inferior if right coronary involved); single BP, not unequal; troponin rises <6 hours. *Splitter: Unequal arm BP + normal ECG → dissection, not STEMI.*
- **Pulmonary embolism** – tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, D-dimer high, CT pulmonary angiogram shows clot, not aortic flap
- **Pneumothorax** – pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, CXR shows air, not widened mediastinum
- **Pericarditis** – positional pleuritic pain, diffuse ST elevation, PR depression, normal aortic imaging
- **Musculoskeletal chest pain** – no haemodynamic instability, reproducible tenderness, normal ECG/imaging

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ECG** – rule out STEMI (but remember: inferior MI can be caused by dissection)
  - **BP both arms** – document difference >20 mmHg
  - **Chest X-ray** – look for widened mediastinum >8 cm at aortic knob, loss of aortic knuckle, pleural cap – but normal CXR does NOT rule out dissection
- **Bloods:**
  - **Troponin** – often negative early; if elevated, suggests coronary involvement
  - **D-dimer** – sensitive (if negative, low risk) but not specific
  - **Lactate** – for end-organ hypoperfusion
  - **Cross-match** – urgent for potential surgery
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT aortogram (CT angiogram with ECG-gating) – gold standard; shows intimal flap, false lumen, extension
  - **Second-line:** Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) – if patient unstable and cannot go to CT; also assesses AR and tamponade
  - **Transthoracic echo (TTE):** poor for ascending aorta but can spot AR, pericardial effusion, proximal flap
- **Never-miss test:** **CT aortogram** – omitting it in a patient with suggestive history + any abnormal finding (pain, pulse deficit, wide mediastinum) is a serious error

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** – secure IV access ×2, monitor BP
  - **Analgesia:** IV morphine 5-10 mg titrated
  - **Heart rate control first:** IV beta-blocker (labetalol or esmolol) to target HR 50-60 bpm before considering vasodilators
  - **Blood pressure control:** If SBP >120 mmHg after HR controlled, add IV vasodilator (e.g. sodium nitroprusside [check local policy] or nicardipine infusion) to target SBP 100-120 mmHg
  - **Urgent referral to cardiothoracic surgery and/or vascular surgery** – type A (involving ascending aorta) needs emergency surgery; type B (descending only) may be medical if uncomplicated
  - **DO NOT give thrombolytics** for suspected STEMI if dissection is possible
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Monitor in HDU/ICU** – strict BP control with continuous arterial line
  - **Type A:** emergency surgical repair (open or endovascular)
  - **Type B (uncomplicated):** continue medical therapy: beta-blocker + calcium channel blocker (if beta-blocker CI); keep SBP <120 mmHg, HR <60
  - **Pain control** – if pain persists, suspect rupture or extension → urgent reassessment
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Lifelong antihypertensives** – beta-blocker first-line (e.g. atenolol 50-100 mg daily), add ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5-10 mg daily) or calcium channel blocker (e.g. amlodipine 5-10 mg daily) to keep SBP <130 mmHg
  - **Regular imaging surveillance** – CT or MRI at 1, 3, 6, 12 months then annually if stable
  - **Conservative lifestyle** – avoid heavy lifting, straining, contact sports
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until surgical plan clear
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 q15min initially; once stable q1h; plus continuous BP and HR
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily – but hold if risk of bleeding (pending surgery, malperfusion)
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid at 100 mL/h unless hypotension (then consider caution – too much fluid can worsen tamponade)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Labetalol** – IV bolus 20 mg over 2 min, then 40-80 mg every 10 min up to 300 mg total, or infusion 1-2 mg/min
  - **Esmolol** – IV loading 500 mcg/kg over 1 min, then infusion 50-200 mcg/kg/min
  - **Metoprolol** – IV 5 mg every 5 min up to 15 mg (if labetalol unavailable)
  - **Sodium nitroprusside** – infusion 0.3-10 mcg/kg/min (use only after beta-blockade)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Diltiazem** IV – if beta-blocker contraindicated (e.g. asthma, cocaine use) – 0.25 mg/kg bolus, then infusion 5-15 mg/h
  - **Nicardipine** – IV infusion 5-15 mg/h (alternative vasodilator)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Cocaine-induced dissection: avoid beta-blockers initially; use calcium channel blocker + benzodiazepine
  - Bradycardia/heart block: caution with beta-blockers
  - Sodium nitroprusside: risk of cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (>24h) or renal impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] – e.g. nitroprusside metabolites accumulate in renal failure; labetalol dose may need reduction in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving a vasodilator (e.g. GTN, nitroprusside) before achieving heart rate <60 bpm with a beta-blocker** – this can cause reflex tachycardia and propagate the dissection

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Rupture** – sudden hypotension, loss of pulse, death
  - **Cardiac tamponade** – due to haemorrhage into pericardium
  - **Acute aortic regurgitation** – pulmonary oedema, hypotension
  - **Myocardial infarction** – due to dissection flap occluding coronary ostium
  - **Stroke** – carotid involvement
  - **Spinal cord ischaemia** – paraplegia
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia** – abdominal pain, lactate rising
  - **Renal artery occlusion** – anuria, rising creatinine
  - **Limb ischaemia** – absent pulse, cold limb
- **Chronic:**
  - **Chronic dissection** – persistent false lumen, risk of late rupture
  - **Aneurysm formation** – up to 40% of survivors develop aneurysm
  - **Recurrent dissection** – especially in hypertensive patients

### Prognosis
- **Type A:** Untreated mortality ~1-2% per hour in first 48h; emergency surgery reduces in-hospital mortality to 10-30%. Survival beyond 5 years ~60% with good BP control.
- **Type B (uncomplicated):** In-hospital mortality <10% with medical management; 5-year survival >70% if BP controlled.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check all pulses (carotid, radial, femoral, dorsalis pedis)** and **measure BP in both arms** on every patient with severe chest/back pain – asymmetry is a red flag
- **Listen for aortic regurgitation** – early diastolic murmur at left lower sternal edge – even if BP is high
- **If you suspect dissection, page cardiothoracic surgery immediately** – do not wait for all investigations to come back

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't rely on a normal chest X-ray** to rule out dissection – up to 20% of type A dissections have a normal CXR
- **Don't wait for troponin to "rule in" MI** before considering dissection – a negative troponin does not exclude dissection
- **Do NOT give thrombolysis** (for STEMI) if there is any suspicion of dissection – catastrophic bleeding

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Sudden severe recurrence of chest/back pain, syncope, new neurological symptoms (focal weakness, hoarseness), or limb pain/pallor
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient vascular/cardiothoracic clinic within 2-4 weeks; arrange surveillance CT/MRI

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (CT surgery / vascular surgery) immediately if:** Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg), acute neurological deterioration, signs of tamponade (hypotension + muffled sounds + JVP↑)
- **Call ICU:** NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS drop by ≥2 points, refractory hypertension (SBP >140 mmHg despite two agents)
- **Crash team:** Cardiorespiratory arrest – consider ED thoracotomy in selected cases (if tamponade/rupture suspected)
